skill.name: enact-hello-brainfuck
skill.description: Runs a fixed Brainfuck program that returns a simple greeting message through an Enact tool.
skill.summary: Execute a small Brainfuck greeting example as an Enact tool.
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby backtick execution are false positives from Markdown inline code and fenced examples. The weak cryptography findings are also false positives because the cited lines describe Brainfuck and greeting examples, not cryptographic code. The real risk is fixed external command execution in the Enact manifest, which installs and runs a Brainfuck interpreter without user-controlled arguments.

medium_findings.title: Fixed External Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The Enact manifest installs the beef package during build and runs beef against /workspace/hello.bf at runtime. The command and file path are fixed, and the input schema has no user properties, so command injection evidence was not found. This still needs a marketplace warning because publishing requires trust in the container package install and interpreter execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The manifest directly shows package installation and runtime command execution. Confidence is high because the command is explicit, but the risk is bounded by fixed arguments and an empty input schema.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick findings point to Markdown examples for Brainfuck syntax, sample code, CLI usage, MCP invocation, and displayed output. These lines are documentation text, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown inline code or fenced documentation examples. No executable Ruby context or user-controlled shell call appears on those lines.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited lines describe the Brainfuck greeting tool, output schema text, an example description, and a closing rationale. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing function, encryption routine, or security claim appears in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contents are plain descriptions and do not implement cryptography. The pattern is very likely a lexical false positive from the word Brainfuck or nearby text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Container Package Install and Runtime Interpreter Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses a build command to install beef and a runtime command to execute a local Brainfuck file. The pattern is operationally legitimate for this skill, but it is still external command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are declared in the manifest and are required for the tool to run. No evidence of dynamic argument construction or data exfiltration was found.

content.value_statement: Teams sometimes need compact examples that prove an agent can call an Enact tool. This skill runs a fixed Brainfuck program and returns a simple greeting.
actual_capabilities: Runs a static Brainfuck source file through the beef interpreter.
Returns a greeting message with no user input required.
Provides an Enact manifest with build, command, timeout, and schemas.
Demonstrates how Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can call an Enact tool.
Includes documentation that explains the Brainfuck commands used.
limitations: It only returns one predefined greeting message.
It does not accept parameters or generate custom Brainfuck programs.
It depends on the beef package during the container build.
It is an example tool, not a general Brainfuck development environment.

use_cases.title: Test Agent Tool Wiring
use_cases.description: Run a minimal tool call to confirm that an agent can invoke an Enact skill and receive structured output.

use_cases.title: Teach Esoteric Language Basics
use_cases.description: Show learners how a tiny Brainfuck program can create a readable greeting through repeated character output.

use_cases.title: Provide a Lightweight Demo Skill
use_cases.description: Use a small greeting tool as a marketplace or registry example with clear inputs and outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Run the Greeting
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check that the tool responds.
prompt_templates.prompt: Call the hello Brainfuck tool and tell me the greeting it returns.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Result
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the hello Brainfuck tool, then explain the returned message in one short paragraph.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect the Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of the tool behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the hello Brainfuck tool and summarize how the fixed program creates the greeting.

prompt_templates.title: Audit the Demo Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether this Brainfuck greeting tool is suitable as a safe demo for agent tool invocation.

output_examples.input: Run the tool with no arguments.
output_examples.output: The tool returns a message that says Hello from Brainfuck.

output_examples.input: Ask for a short explanation after running the tool.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the greeting and explains that a fixed Brainfuck program produced it.

output_examples.input: Use it as an agent integration smoke test.
output_examples.output: The agent confirms that the tool call completed and produced the expected greeting.

best_practices: Use it as a small integration check, not as a production workflow.
Review the container build step before allowing publication in restricted environments.
Keep the input schema empty unless the runtime command is redesigned safely.
anti_patterns: Do not treat this as a general Brainfuck interpreter for user-submitted programs.
Do not add dynamic shell arguments without strict validation and escaping.
Do not rely on it for security, cryptography, or data processing tasks.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It runs a fixed Brainfuck program and returns a simple greeting message.

faq.question: Does it require input?
faq.answer: No. The input schema has no properties, so the tool runs without arguments.

faq.question: Is it a full Brainfuck interpreter?
faq.answer: No. It uses the beef interpreter to run one bundled Brainfuck source file.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill installs a package and runs an external interpreter command, even though the arguments are fixed.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Should users pass custom code to it?
faq.answer: No. The published skill is designed for one bundled greeting program only.
